g.data-narration: Demand paging loads pages into memory only when they are needed. A page is brought from disk to memory only when a process accesses it. Lazy approach to loading.
g.text: Load pages only when accessed, never load unnecessary pages

g.data-narration: Valid-invalid bit in page table: valid means page is in memory. Invalid means page is on disk or illegal. When process accesses invalid page, page fault trap occurs.
g.text: i = page on disk or illegal (invalid)
Access to 'i' page = page fault trap

g.data-narration: Page fault handling steps: trap to OS, save process state, find page on disk, find free frame, read page into frame, update page table, restart instruction that caused fault.
Pure demand paging: start process with no pages in memory. Every first access to a page causes a fault. High initial fault rate but levels off as working set loads.
g.text: Start with zero pages in memory
Fault rate decreases as pages load

g.data-narration: Effective access time: EAT equals one minus p times memory access plus p times page fault service time. If p is 0.001 and fault takes 8ms, EAT is about 8 microseconds. Must keep p low.
g.text: EAT = (1-p) x mem_access + p x fault_time
Must keep page fault rate p very low

g.data-narration: Hardware support required: page table with valid-invalid bit, secondary memory or swap space for pages, and ability to restart any instruction after page fault is serviced.
No free frame available? Must use page replacement algorithm to choose victim page. Write victim to disk if modified (dirty bit set). Load needed page into freed frame.
g.text: Dirty bit: write back only if modified

g.data-narration: Demand paging loads pages lazily on first access. Page faults trigger disk reads. Effective access time depends on keeping fault rate extremely low through good replacement policies.
g.text: Lazy Loading + Page Faults + Low Fault Rate = Efficient Demand Paging
